full_transcript.text: I'm actually looking forward to that. I mean, I've been involved in a lot of litigation over the years. I'm sure you've been involved in more. And so would love to hear that thought because I'm- hopefully this- this video is not an exhibit A in the original fifth thing that- that, you know, this is. But- but yeah, so you go for it.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No, no. Give your- give your thought and then I'll- I'll give...

full_transcript.speaker: John Arrow
full_transcript.text: I think there's existing laws, right? There's publicity laws. If you look at different states, a lot of them have laws, you know, Walt Disney copyright, that's which is separate from publicity. But a lot of laws, like Texas, where- where, you know, I'm based. I spend my time between Texas and New York. They have 60 years, right? So basically for 60 years after someone's death, unless they explicitly gave that right away, you're not allowed to do that. So it doesn't matter if it's AI. There's already an existing law on the books that would prohibit that. Other states go longer, other states go shorter. But if you were out there in the public saying 'I pledge this right, I donate this right', then you are the estate. You are part of that then.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So, there is a law called the right to publicity. And this is for famous people. How do I know about this law? Sometimes people will take me, or an AI version of me, and they will start selling their crypto project or some nutrient and I have to have my team go file and just write a letter and say 'Hey, don't do that.' I've had this happen many times. Somebody did it in one of their promotional videos famously on the internet and I said 'Hey, please take me out of that.' Why? Because you're infringing on my ability to do endorsements in other places. People pay me six and embarrassingly even seven figures for my endorsement. That's the reality of where I am in my career. It took 25- no, 30 years of me building my career to be able to get those opportunities. Now, uh, and so what they did was they removed me from their endorsement and then they put a puppet in and they republished it and they got even more. And I was like 'Listen guys, I'm not going to sue you. Just- please just leave me out of it.' And then they had some fun with me, which is totally fine. This right is done on a state-by-state level. It's not done at the federal level. And the reason this- uh, exists is because you don't want commercial exploitation and the people who have passed away or are alive have a business in this. So the estate of Elvis has a massive licensing business and they will come down on you just like Mickey Mouse. But Mickey Mouse is a fictional character. You know, for a real character like Elvis, you basically get 75-100 years from the time of death to- uh, exploit that person's IP. You can't use it for confusing people, etc. But there is a protected area. Editorial use like a documentary. You can make a documentary about Scott Adams right now without permission, or Elvis. Artistic use. You could make an artistic- you could make an entire album...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: of songs as a tribute to Scott Adams and his work. You could do any type of news reporting and if somebody happened to be like if you had a picture of me in the background of a photo, you know, at a demo day and there were 20 of us there, you don't have to pay for that. But that's why when like Steve Jobs did his famous ad about the misfits and everything, each of those people in that commercial use had to be approved. So when he said the artists, the misfits, and they show Bob Dylan, or the Beatles, or Einstein, whoever's under 100 years old that has an estate, you do. So here's what's going to happen I predict with John and Scott Adams. You'll either move to an editorially protected area that doesn't infringe on the actual family's ability to do that, which would be making it more artistic, making sure it's non-commercial and making sure it doesn't confuse the users, and you will then be protected. Or you'll have a heart-to-heart with the family and say, hey, we're going to make Abigail, and Abigail's an inspiration based on Scott's work, and she's the female version who brings the Gaia to Scott Adams' work. And it'd be like super cool because it's an interpretation, it's artistic, etc. Or you'll sit down, break bread with the family, and the family will give you a license to it. And they'll say under these circumstances and these tools, we don't want you to do anything adult or inappropriate or racist or this or that. We don't want you to bring up the cancellation where Scott got canceled for what he said or whatever. We just want it to live in this zone and have the ability to approve it, and any money goes to our children. I can tell you right now, my estate is going to defend this to the end of the earth, my 2,500 podcast episodes and growing, so that my three daughters get every fucking penny of everything because I'm not giving any money to charity when I go. I'm giving it to my three daughters to go rule the universe and be queens and take over the empire. Full stop.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: We are getting a lot of pushback from the chat, Jason, I feel like I should mention. A lot of folks in the chat upset with our depiction. Okay, rapid fire. Uh, so at DrDavis62, Scott Adams spent the last year explaining on air that he would not create an AI Scott Adams because he did not feel that the tech was up to the task. Uh, LidoOpa, the age of the AI agent is akin to ethical slavery. Uh, another DrDavis, he did not want to be memorialized in this way and said so on his podcast. He looked at the tech and felt that it would misrepresent him. Uh, so John, do you want to respond to these people who feel like Scott was clear at the end of his career that he didn't actually want an AI clone?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: End of his life.

full_transcript.speaker: John Arrow
full_transcript.text: Absolutely. First, let me respond to what Jason said. I'm with you, Jason, and I would love to have that type of conversation. There's, you know, there's been no attempt to have it. Any of my attempts to have it have kind of fallen on deaf ears. So I'd love to have that conversation. Second of all, the question I would pose for everybody that kind of is in line with those comments is if you're somebody who wants to pledge your existence so that people can make AIs out of you, how do you do it? Because Scott Adams did it, tried dozens of times, and said, this is—

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: This is what I want. This is what I want. And despite some of those comments, there is not one video segment of him revoking it or saying, look, don't do this anymore. I know I said this over and over and over again, I pledge my likeness, everything about me to the public domain. Go out there and make AIs. That's what he said, there's many videos of him saying that, no videos of him not saying that. So how would somebody, how would somebody go out and even do that? I mean, that's the question I would pose for everybody else because if you, I signed up, if I die, hopefully I'm immortal, but if I die, I'm signed up to go to Alcor where I'm frozen out in, I think it's in Scottsdale. You know, and it's kind of a scary thing if people love and care about you, they might decide no, John doesn't really want that, right? And normally we have wills and last testaments for that type of thing, but Scott did something even better. He said that to the public. And that does constitute a pledge and something that he wanted to do. So for all these people, you know, filling up your comments saying he didn't want this, this sounds a lot like mind reading to me. And it's something that Scott Adams warned against. Don't try to predict what other people want, go by what their words are.

full_transcript.speaker: John Arrow
full_transcript.text: Okay. All the corpsicles are in Arizona? Why would we put all the frozen people in the hottest part of the United States?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I think it's less... less of earthquakes.

full_transcript.speaker: John Arrow
full_transcript.text: Like, I mean, Arizona's made for this.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Or Alaska?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I think it must have to do with laws. I gotta think it has to do with laws as well.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Anyway, this here's... you know, Scott had a concept of internet dads, and I think I was part of that group of, like, you know, Gen X dads or as people are starting to call me Unk. I guess that's like a way of saying boomer, they call me Unk. Yeah, they call me Unk now. I think Hiten, you're going to get into the Unk category pretty soon. Lon, you're there. So here's what Unk says. I'm going to give my... as the chairman of the interwebs, I'm going to give my... what is it when a judge gives their statement, Lon?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Their ruling.

full_transcript.speaker: John Arrow
full_transcript.text: Verdict?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Ruling. Verdict? Ruling. I'm going to give my ruling. Here's my ruling. Ruling. My ruling is: Scott would have loved this. Scott would have loved it. And Scott would have had some space that if it was hurting his family members and it was traumatizing for them, if it was too soon, a month after his death, or the fidelity wasn't there, or they didn't have approval of it, he would have also taken that into account. As such, I am laying my judgment down. John, you got to break bread with the family. And if you're going to make a literal one, you have to then get their permission. However, if John wants to make one that is a send-up, parody, female version, a Muppet version, that is extremely clear to the public that this is not Scott Adams, it is an inspiration of Scott's philosophy carrying on in the future, that there is space for that as well. And that means it would have to have another name, and, you know, in the fine print say, hey, this is inspired by our love for Scott Adams.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That is my ruling, and uh you can appeal it. The appeal can happen at any time by any person, and we will thank John for coming to the program and to the family of Scott Adams. What a tremendous career, we miss him. Um, him- he and I debated things, I was on his show once, we had crazy debates about January 6th and all kinds of things, but I think he was a net-net positive for the world and actually did actually care about people. So rest in peace, power, replicant Scott Adams. We'll drop you off John.

full_transcript.speaker: Jesse Genet
full_transcript.text: Yeah, peace- peace is good.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You are a star Jesse, thanks for coming. Hiten, John-

full_transcript.speaker: Jesse Genet
full_transcript.text: I had so much fun!

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Hiten, John, thank you for coming.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Hiten, John, you could drop off. We will see you on the next episode. Well done, well done. Plenty in the show notes about those great guests we had.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: His name is, and he's exhausted because he was up late last night watching movies or doing open-clo-, his name is Lon Harris, follow him x.com/lons, lons. He's Alex, x.com/alex. They're part of the four-letter club on Twitter. I'm right behind him, x.com/jason. I've got more work to do, if I could get rid of the 'a' and just be JSON.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: JSON, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: We'll see you all on Wednesday for another live insane unstoppable twist. Bye-bye.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Bye.

highlights: OpenAI’s acquisition of the open-source project OpenClaw signals a shift toward an "interface war," where owning the user’s primary entry point to AI is becoming more strategic than the underlying models themselves.

The rise of "solopreneur billion-dollar companies" is increasingly feasible as AI agents allow non-technical founders to build complex applications, such as personalized homeschooling curricula and competitive intelligence tools, entirely through natural language.

Software is moving toward an "app-less" and "UI-less" future where traditional visual interfaces are replaced by direct interactions with autonomous agents that manage data and execute tasks across various platforms.

The creation of an AI clone for the late Scott Adams highlights emerging legal and ethical debates regarding "digital immortality" and whether a person's public authorization of their likeness can override the control of their estate.
summary:
This transcript from a February 2026 episode of *This Week in Startups* centers on the breaking news of OpenAI’s acquisition of the open-source sensation **OpenClaw**. Hosts Jason Calacanis and Alex Wilhelm discuss the strategic implications of the deal, which is estimated to be worth mid-to-high nine figures. While some view the acquisition as a "rug pull" of a promising open-source project, the discussion weighs two perspectives: a cynical view that Sam Altman is neutralizing an interface that threatened to commoditize OpenAI, and an optimistic view that the project’s move to a foundation led by Dave Morin will provide it with necessary resources. Calacanis emphasizes the need for a "counter-strategy," pledging to fund startups that focus on keeping OpenClaw's security, hosting, and skills open and decentralized.

The conversation shifts to the practical revolution triggered by OpenClaw, featuring guests Hiten Shah and Jesse Genet, who demonstrate how AI agents—or "replicants"—are replacing traditional software and user interfaces. Jesse Genet showcases how she used OpenClaw to build a custom, "slop-free" YouTube app for her children and an automated homeschooling curriculum, leading Calacanis to offer her an immediate $125,000 investment. Hiten Shah demonstrates how he built a personal CRM in minutes and maintains autonomous dashboards. A major theme of this segment is the "death of the UI," as founders move toward direct, text- or voice-based interactions with agents that possess the "agency" to perform complex professional tasks.

The final segment explores the ethical and legal frontier of **"digital immortality"** through a project created by John Arrow: an AI clone of the late cartoonist Scott Adams. The project sparked significant controversy after being banned by YouTube for potential viewer confusion. The discussion explores the "Right to Publicity" and the moral tension between a creator’s public wish to be memorialized via AI and their estate’s desire for privacy. The hosts and guests debate the need for "Do Not Replicate" (DNR) legal filings and how the falling cost of AI will lead to more frequent, unauthorized recreations of public figures, ultimately concluding that "break bread" negotiations with families are essential for such high-fidelity digital replicas.
